paper_title: Mechanical properties of Fe–Al–M–C (MZTi, V, Nb, Ta) alloys with strengthening carbides and Laves phase
citation_sentences: There is a good correlation between hardness and yield stress in compression for Fe–15Al based alloys with Ti and V (Fig. 1a). On the other hand, the alloy Fe–15Al–2Nb–1C has higher hardness, but lower yield stress than the alloy Fe–15Al–2Ta–1C.

The matrix hardness values of most Fe–26Al based alloys do not correlate with values of yield stress in compression (Fig. 1b). Except alloy Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C, all other Fe–26Al based alloys exhibit lower yield stress in compression than the binary Fe–26Al alloy.

caption: Fig. 2. Engineering stress–strain curves of Fe–15Al-based (a) and Fe–26Albased (b) alloys tested at room temperature in compression.
paper_abstract: Max-Planck-Institut fu¨r Eisenforschung GmbH, Max-Planck-Str. 1, Du¨sseldorf D-40237, Germany
paper_title: Mechanical properties of Fe–Al–M–C (MZTi, V, Nb, Ta) alloys with strengthening carbides and Laves phase
citation_sentences: The deformation behaviour of Fe–26Al–(2 or 4)M–1C alloys (Fig. 2b) exhibits very high strengthening with almost constant hardening rates, until a final stage of parabolic work hardening is reached. The stress–strain curve of the binary Fe–26Al alloy consist of a linear elastic part, followed by a parabolic hardening stage. Despite lower yield stresses of most Fe–26Al based alloys in comparison to the binary alloy, all these alloys exhibit much stronger hardening effects during plastic deformation.

paper_title: Mechanical properties of Fe–Al–M–C (MZTi, V, Nb, Ta) alloys with strengthening carbides and Laves phase
citation_sentences: In Fig. 4, SEM-fractographs of investigated alloys are shown. The binary Fe–15Al alloy with ductility of 16%, was fractured with transgranular fracture with very fine cleavage facets (Fig. 4a). All Fe–15Al–2M–1C alloys were fractured with coarse transgranular cleavage (Fig. 4b–e), which corresponds to their brittle behaviour. The alloy Fe–15Al– 2Ti–1C exhibits the highest room-temperature ductility (z3.3%) of all Fe–15Al-based alloys. The binary Fe–26Al alloy exhibits a very coarse transgranular fracture (Fig. 4f). The alloy Fe–26Al–2Nb–1C which has the highest ductility (z1.4%) of all Fe–26Al based alloys, exhibits a very fine facetted transgranular cleavage (Fig. 4j). The fracture surface of the alloy Fe–26Al–2V–1C with uniform steps indicates transgranular fracture (Fig. 4i), with the influence of the crystallographic orientation of plate-like vanadium carbide precipitates. The orientation relationship of these vanadium carbide precipitates with the matrix was determined in a previous study [21]. According to this result, it can be stated that the fracture facets are formed on the (001) planes of the matrix which are parallel to the (001) planes of the precipitates. For the other Fe–26Al-based alloys a more complex fracture was observed. The fracture surfaces of the alloys with Ti or Ta are partially intergranular and transgranular (Fig. 4g–l). The smooth fracture areas correspond to the decohesion and the facetted areas to the cleavage. On the intergranular fracture areas of the alloys Fe–26Al–2Ti–1C and Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C the cube-like TiC precipitates can be clearly distinguished. The alloy Fe–26Al– 4Ti–1C was fractured almost completely with intergranular decohesion. The alloy Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C showed intergranular decohesion besides transgranular cleavage (Fig. 4k). On the intergranular fracture areas many densely distributed precipitates are observed. These precipitates disturbed the intergranular fracture surface so significantly that the decohesion areas are not smooth as usually but rough. The alloys Fe–26Al–4Ti–1C and Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C show totally brittle behaviour at room temperature.

caption: Fig. 5. The temperature dependency of the yield stress in compression for Fe–15Al-based alloys.
paper_abstract: Max-Planck-Institut fu¨r Eisenforschung GmbH, Max-Planck-Str. 1, Du¨sseldorf D-40237, Germany
paper_title: Mechanical properties of Fe–Al–M–C (MZTi, V, Nb, Ta) alloys with strengthening carbides and Laves phase
citation_sentences: In order to reveal the potential of investigated alloys as possible high temperature materials, the alloys were tested at elevated temperatures up to $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ Fig. 5 shows the temperature dependency of the yield stress in compression for the Fe–15Al-based alloys. The yield stresses of all Fe–15Al-based alloys show a gradual monotonous decrease with increasing temperature. The alloys with Nb or Ta show the highest strength at all tested temperatures. Fig. 6 shows the temperature dependencies of the yield stress in compression for Fe–26Al-based alloys. All Fe–26Al-based alloys exhibit an anomalous increase in yield strength within a certain range of intermediate temperatures. The temperature at which the peak occurs is about $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and the yield stress at the peak maximum is about 400 MPa for the alloys with Ti and V (Fig. 6a). The yield stress anomaly peak of the Fe–26Al-based alloys with Nb or Ta is shifted towards lower temperatures and higher stresses in comparison to the binary Fe–26Al alloy (Fig. 6b). The alloy Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C exhibits the most significant shift of the peak position $( 4 7 4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , 619 MPa). This alloy has the highest yield stresses at all tested temperatures, which clearly demonstrates the strengthening effect of the Laves phase (Fe, Al)2Nb. Precipitation strengthening of the Fe–Al matrix through MC carbides was found to be also effective for all alloy compositions under investigation. In addition, strengthening through MC carbides is of high importance because of high temperature stability and higher resistance to coarsening in comparison to the Laves phase.

caption: Fig. 6. Temperature dependencies of the yield stress in compression for Fe–26Al-based alloys.

citation_sentences: In order to reveal the potential of investigated alloys as possible high temperature materials, the alloys were tested at elevated temperatures up to $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ Fig. 5 shows the temperature dependency of the yield stress in compression for the Fe–15Al-based alloys. The yield stresses of all Fe–15Al-based alloys show a gradual monotonous decrease with increasing temperature. The alloys with Nb or Ta show the highest strength at all tested temperatures. Fig. 6 shows the temperature dependencies of the yield stress in compression for Fe–26Al-based alloys. All Fe–26Al-based alloys exhibit an anomalous increase in yield strength within a certain range of intermediate temperatures. The temperature at which the peak occurs is about $5 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ and the yield stress at the peak maximum is about 400 MPa for the alloys with Ti and V (Fig. 6a). The yield stress anomaly peak of the Fe–26Al-based alloys with Nb or Ta is shifted towards lower temperatures and higher stresses in comparison to the binary Fe–26Al alloy (Fig. 6b). The alloy Fe–26Al–4Nb–1C exhibits the most significant shift of the peak position $( 4 7 4 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } ,$ , 619 MPa). This alloy has the highest yield stresses at all tested temperatures, which clearly demonstrates the strengthening effect of the Laves phase (Fe, Al)2Nb. Precipitation strengthening of the Fe–Al matrix through MC carbides was found to be also effective for all alloy compositions under investigation. In addition, strengthening through MC carbides is of high importance because of high temperature stability and higher resistance to coarsening in comparison to the Laves phase.

caption: Fig. 7. Yield stresses at $6 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ in compression and in tension for Fe–15Albased (a) and Fe–26Al-based (b) alloys.

paper_title: Mechanical properties of Fe–Al–M–C (MZTi, V, Nb, Ta) alloys with strengthening carbides and Laves phase
citation_sentences: Since the mechanical testing of alloys at room temperature revealed a significant tension–compression asymmetry, the alloys were tested also in tension at 650 and $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ , which are regarded as possible application temperatures. The comparison of the yield stress in compression and in tension at $6 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Fig. 7) as well as at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Fig. 8) confirms the asymmetric tension–compression behaviour of all investigated alloys at both temperatures.

caption: Fig. 8. Yield stresses at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ in compression and in tension for Fe–15Albased (a) and Fe–26Al-based (b) alloys.

paper_title: Mechanical properties of Fe–Al–M–C (MZTi, V, Nb, Ta) alloys with strengthening carbides and Laves phase
citation_sentences: Since the mechanical testing of alloys at room temperature revealed a significant tension–compression asymmetry, the alloys were tested also in tension at 650 and $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C } .$ , which are regarded as possible application temperatures. The comparison of the yield stress in compression and in tension at $6 5 0 ~ ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Fig. 7) as well as at $8 0 0 ^ { \circ } \mathrm { C }$ (Fig. 8) confirms the asymmetric tension–compression behaviour of all investigated alloys at both temperatures.
